ocr_text:
Is Gold Reflecting Commodity Costs?
article on link between record gold and silver prices and effects on commodity costs; says some commodity experts believe recent advance in precious metals are merely tail of inflationary forces unleashed by past rises in raw-materials costs; says others believe rise in metals means future increases of raw materials since price of precious metals is inverse gauge of value of paper money; notes bankers fear potentially disastrous effects that rising metal prices could mean in form of inflation; graph shows link between commodities and gold, Jan '72-Feb '74 (L)

Earnings of Ford Tumbled 76% in Final '73 Quarter
Ford Motor Co repts its earnings for '73 4th qr were $57-million, compared with $240-million in '72 corresponding period; repts net income for '73 was record $907-million, compared wtih $870-million in '72; graph compares Ford's total revenue and net income from '64 to 73

Auto Boom Amid Signs Of Revolt
article on major problems confronting auto indus in '74 notes that there is possibility of consumer revolt against 'burdensome' auto-safety equipment

LEADING INDEXES UP 1.7% IN MARCH;Dent Says Gain Indicates Economy Will Improve—Some Are Doubtful
Potlatch Corp on April 26 approves investments of more than $100-million for 140,000-ton pulp and paperboard mill in south-eastern Arkansas

Junta's Harsh Economic Measures Stagger Chileans
article, discussing Chile's mil junta's program for econ stability, notes that workers will bear brunt of efforts to increase indus production; workweek has already been lengthened from 44 hrs to 48 hrs; workers not inspired by patriotic calls for 'natl reconstruction' are cowed by possibility of losing their jobs; so-called 'extremists' have been dismissed at hundreds of factories, and all union activity has been prohibited; unemployment looms as critical problem; jobless rate in greater Santiago area under Allende was lowered from 8% to less than 4% by creation of thousands of fictional posts in Govt enterprises and agencies

WINE TALK
Article on Simi Winery head winemaker Mary Ann Graf, who is probably only woman in US to hold that title; discusses functions of head winemaker; co owner Russell Green comments on Grafs ability; Graf illus (L)

Measles Outbreak Strikes At Teen‐Agers in Bergen
NJ Acting Health Comr Dr William J Dougherty repts 252 new cases of measles, 169 more than usual, from Sept '73 through Mar '74 in NJ; says outbreak is spreading rapidly and affecting teenagers, particularly in Bergen County; cases reptd in various areas noted; Dr Thomas M Drew, health dept epidemiologist, holds disease becomes more severe as affected person gets older; health dept reptdly has distributed over 100,000 cases of vaccine since Sept; Paterson Mayor Thomas Rooney notes disease is on wane in city (M)

Simon a Firm Believer in Free‐Enterprise, Free‐Market System With a Minimal Government Role
Treas Sec designate William E Simon is convinced believer in free-mkt system with govt interference kept to minimum; his philosophy discussed (L)

Low Italian Dudgeon
R Baker humorous article on Columbus Day

MAYOR ACTS TO PAY TOWN FOR HER LIFE
Mrs C Robinson, Mayor of Metter, Ga, says she ran for office to repay town's citizens for saving her life; Robinson, first woman Mayor in town's history, underwent surgery for terminal cancer 5 yrs ago; drs recently told Robinson, who has been Mayor for almost 1 yr, that her cancer had returned; she has been undergoing chemotherapy treatments for 8 wks, and drs tell her that they will know in another mo whether treatments have been successful
GROWING DEFICITS EXPECTED IN WAKE OF OIL‐PRICE RISE

GROWING DEFICITS EXPECTED IN WAKE OF OIL‐PRICE RISE
Accelerating recessionary forces coupled with further painful increases in cost of living are foreseen Dec 24 in W Eur and in other industrialized and developing countries in wake of doubling of price of crude oil by major Persian Gulf exporting states; EEC comm estimates prolonged oil crisis could double unemployment and plunge Eur into deep recession; situation in specific countries discussed

The Economic Scene
article on consumer spending and consumer confidence cites evidence that both are losing their resiliency; notes that since consumer spending is single most important sector in econ, continued and sustained trend could take steam out of the economic boiler; holds that a gradual and moderate decline in buying, however, could be constructive in an inflation-beset econ

New Housing Starts Fall; Personal Income Higher
Former chmn of Econ Advisers Council P W McCracken, M K Evans of Chase Econometric Assocs and P A Samuelson of MIT testify at hearings by Cong Joint Econ Com in its periodic reassessment of econ; McCracken says econ outlook is better than indicated by sour attitude of much of public, but that prospects for continued inflation, particularly in indus, may be worse than most people think; sees '74 business investment limited more by ability of capital-goods industries to deliver than by demand; sees unlikely any large shift to inventory liquidation; says inventories would have to go up some $20-billion to approximate conditions of past downturns; Evans says there is virtually no chance that '74 slowdown will not develop on schedule; Samuelson voices fear that Adm will be stampeded by food-price inflation to adopt drastically restrictive fiscal and monetary policies that could usher in serious period of stagnation

The Economy
Article on whether econ can keep growing, producing jobs and profit, when future of pol leadership is in grave doubt; stock mkt past wk seemed to be saying 'no'; Econ Advisers Council member O Eckstein, in his latest econ anal, says instability at center of natl power poses major risk to '74 econ; says experience of other nations repeatedly demonstrates that econ is adversely affected when durability and continuity of natl authority is in doubt; drawing

Exodus Clouds City Business Outlook
Survey of NYC businessmen shows that most believe Mayor-elect Beame's biggest challenge will be to improve econ climate of NYC; reprs of garment indus want financial incentives to keep business in city; number of garment workers in NYC declined from 238,800 to 186,300 during 1960s; S C Chaikin, gen sec-treas of ILGWU, says tax abatements are needed to attract garment indus businesses to locate in indus pks closer to workers' homes; says moving factories out of mid-Manhattan garment dist would free center to become exclusively showroom center; says city has lost 5,000 visiting buyers in last 5 yrs; por of Chaikin

Numismatics
Article holds that US Bur of the Mint is studying problem of a substitute for increasingly expensive copper that makes up 95% of 1c coin, and is also launching long-range probe into how many coins of all denominations are likely to be needed in everyday commerce 2 yrs, or even 20 yrs, from now; inauguration of 2 studies was recently reptd by Mint dir M T Brooks through Treas Dept; Brooks, apprearing as guest speaker at luncheon meeting of Natl Automatic Merchandising Assn, Washington, DC, last mo, urged officials of vending machine indus to conduct surveys of their business outlook for purpose of determining future coin usage requirements; asserted that information will be of vital importance in helping Bur to more accurately meet future coin demands; cited meeting of new Bicentennial coinage law that calls for Amer Revolution commemorative designs; discussed Mint's plans for new branch in Denver to be completed in '80; reptd that Coinage Study Group has been set up in Treas Dept, and 1 assignment is to study fluctuating prices in metal mkts which may necessitate substitution of less costly materials--not necessarily metallic--in 1 or more coins

U.S BUSINESS ROUND‐UP
Agr expert J H McManigal recently declared that over past 5 yrs farmland has proved to be better investment than stock or bonds, noting that recent surge in land prices has been stimulated mainly by extraordinary gains in farm income; net farm income is forecast as record $24-billion in '73, 20% higher than '72; illus

In Aerospace,Throttling Back
oil shortage seen slowing growth of aerospace industries; impact of shortage on aircraft indus noted; illus (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

BURLINGTON HEAD MAY LEAVE EARLY
Charles F Myers jr, chmn of Burlington Industries Inc, tells stockholders he is considering early retirement but would remain dir of co; Horace C Jones, pres and chief exec officer, is expected to become chmn; Jones tells meeting that Burlington is optimistic about '74 business outlook; co reptd 12% gain in sales and 48% rise in earnings for 1st fiscal qr ended Dec 29, '73 (M)
News Summary and Index

News Summary and Index
correction; Feb 12 article described Mrs Alice Roosevelt Longworth as Pres T Roosevelt's only daughter; Pres had 2 daughters: Longworth by his 1st marriage and Mrs Ethel Roosevelt Derby by his 2d (S)

Business Brief
dollar fluctuates on Eur money mkts on Apr 11; dollar rates against French, Belgian, Brit, and Austrian currencies noted; gold prices vary on Eur bullion mkts, closing at $174.50 an ounce in London and $170.50 on Zurich mkt (S)
ANALYSTS SEE FED EASING ITS POLICY

Real G.N.P. Growth Was3.6% in Quarter
Commerce Dept repts 3d qr real growth in GNP was 3.6%; actual annual rate, without eliminating effects of inflation, was $1,304-billion, a growth of $32-billion or 10.4%; real growth rate compares with smaller 2.4% rate in 2d qr; inflation rate in GNP is at annual rate of 6.7% in 3d qr compared with 7.3% in 2d qr; chart showing GNP and inflation, '63 to 3d qr of '73; Asst Sec of Commerce S L Jones welcomes slowdown and says he would be happy with a growth rate of some 3.4% in 4th qr; says there still are strong inflationary pressures which he believes will continue; Fed Reserve Bd repts mfg plants operated at 83.4% of capacity in the 3d qr, slightly above 83.3% in 2d qr; repts index of mfg output rose to 126.5 from 124.8; repts indus capacity index rose to 151.6 from 149.8

BUSINESS ROUND‐UP
bond specialists are pondering impact that higher gasoline prices and gas shortages might have on toll rds, which have been built by floating huge bond issues; L F Rothschild & Co analyst D H Stevens says that modest reduction in motor vehicle traffic will make it difficult for some tpk auths to maintain interest payments on debt; cites latest rept of ailing Chesapeake Bay Bridge and Tunnel Dist that points up danger; illus

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Comment on econ outlook; virtually all forecasters now see period of small growth in '74, but no recession; many economists warn against complacency because of recent declines in prices; stress need for continuing tight rein on monetary and fiscal policies; majority also back prompt termination of wage-price controls; many share view of Mellon Bank economist N Robertson: 'The longer Phase 4 remains in place, the greater the dangers of distortions, dislocations and shortages, which can only serve to prolong and even intensify the inflationary spiral'; '74 outlook seen as no occasion for gloom; view or former head of Economic Advisers Council P W McCracken cited
Forecast: Partly Sunny

14% Rise in Capital Outlays Is Expected
McGraw-Hill survey to be released Nov 9 shows indus plans to spend record $113.9-billion in '74 for new plant and equipment, 14% above estimated spending in '73; results of survey do not take into acct uncertain effects of oil shortage and energy allocations; survey, made in Oct, shows 2/3 of businessmen believe real GNP will grow 1 to 3.4% in '74, 30% see normal growth of 3.5% or more, only 3% see recession; 25 of 26 major industries expect to increase dollar sales, 3 of 26 see drop in sales; D Greenwald, chief economist for McGraw-Hill, says many of those surveyed believe price controls will end early in '74; says capital spending should be source of strength in econ outlook despite effects of inflation barring drastic impact of energy restrictions; data on projected spending in '74 in various indus sectors; Greenwald says 14% rise in spending represents spillover of '73 spending into '74 because of shortages on supply side, lack of materials and skilled labor

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
comment on '73 as yr of crisis notes present concern in business world about energy crisis; cites Nixon's call for conservation, need for developing untapped sources and expediting expansion of atomic energy

Industrial‐Output Gain Eases
Energy crisis brings number of econ forecasts of lower output, higher unemployment and rise in rate of inflation

ENERGY MEN FIND NIXON PLAN WEAK
T C Gaines, chief economist of Mfrs Hanover Trust Co, predicts real growth rate of near zero in '74, partially because of energy shortage; says Pres Nixon's energy program will do little to improve this gen econ outlook; L H Olsen, chief economist of First Natl City Bank, warns against too much pessimism about the economy due to energy shortage; says no one knows what the impact of shortage will be; says some of the figures presented have not been fully thought out; repr of Continental Illinois Natl Bank & Trust Co says says energy shortages will not be severe, and there will be no recession given reasonable public policies and some voluntary private restraint

Who's running this thing? Nixonomics
Article by Prof R Lekachman, entitled Nixonomics, revs econ policies of Pres Nixon and describes current state of the econ as 'a mess'; cites views of Prof O Eckstein of Harvard, S I Nakagama of Kidder Peabody & Co, T C Gaines of Manufacturers Hanover Trust, Dr P McCracken, former chmn of Econ Advisers Council, and Dr A M Okun; cartoons

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
article discusses private and Govt economists' views on econ outlook for '74 in light of energy shortage; suggests that economists may be unduly minimizing possible econ consequences of harsh brandishment of oil weapon by Arab nations since Eur and Japan may be severely affected by Arab cutbacks, thereby exerting considerable influence on Amer econ; notes public concern over energy situation has been reflected most acutely in steep decline in stock mkt and Adm concern is being reflected in series of moves toward allocation and conservation programs and efforts to start development of alternative sources

Corporate Bond List Again Registers Price Drops
corporate bond dealers on Dec 5, commenting on mkt decline, say losses reflect econ uncertainty in face of energy problems

South Africans End Gold Pact; Little Effect on Prices Expected
S Africa and IMF announce on Dec 7 termination of '69 gold sales agreement; 1st reaction is that there should be little if any effect on gold price, which edges up about $1 an ounce to $107 in quiet trading in London and Zurich; terminated accord underwrote output of S African mines by assuring that their production could always be sold at official price; S Africa was obliged in turn, when it needed to sell gold in private mkt, to do so in 'orderly manner'; announcement, which is issued in Washington, DC, and S Africa, says that initiative for termination came from S Africa
News Summary and Index

Growth Slower in Industrial Production
Fed Reserve econ rept does not mention impact of shortage on econ because officials say they have found no links, except in voluntary reduction

No Real Harm to U.S. Seen in Oil Cuts
OECD predicts on Dec 20 that Arab oil embargo will not seriously impair Amer indus production in '74 because Arab sources fill only 6% of Amer energy consumption and US use of oil for nonproductive purposes is extremely high and could be cut by as much as 15% without significantly affecting productive capacity

POINT OF VIEW
W W Ebanks article discusses flaws in Employment Act of '71, which provides Govt jobs; discusses relationship between Govt's 'involvement' in providing jobs in public sector and its effect on private sector; holds Nixon Adm view is that it is all right for state and local govts to increase employment with Fed money as long as Fed employment does not expand; drawing

U.S. Advantage in Oil Helps Lift Dollar
Sharply higher values for dollar against leading currencies on foreign exch mkts on Dec 27 are considered further indications that devaluations may be offing to give Eur competitive edge against US in world trade

U.S. Advantage in Oil Helps Lift Dollar
stronger position of US, relative to other countries, in wake of recent sharp increases in price of crude oil, is translated on Dec 27 into sharply higher values for dollar against leading currencies; in Frankfurt, Amer currency breaks through what had been considered major resistance level, while in Paris, something of gold rush develops, reflecting fears that franc might soon become one of Eur's weakest currencies; currency movements illustrate dramatically how energy crisis has altered mkt patterns; in several mos, much-disparged dollar, at times even refused for exchange by Eur banks, has again become most sought-after currency in circulation; reasoning behind generalized buying of dollar is that although Amers do suffer from energy crisis, they suffer less than most; one reason for dollar's power is improvement in Amer trading acct following 2 devaluations, which many again see as only start of accelerating trend; cloudy econ outlook for France, combined with large trade deficit expected in '74, and possibility that Socialist Govt may come to power after more than 15 yrs of Gaullism, are among factors weighing on franc; closing rates of dollar against currencies of France, West Ger, and GB noted; Dec 27 actions in foreign exch mkts are considered further indications that devaluations may be in offing to give Eur and Japan competitive edge against US in world trade

Impact of Arabs' Oil Tactics
article details possible impact on Western Eur of Persian Gulf producers' price increase and Arabs 10% production increase; discusses problems that oil-consuming nations face in attempting to pay for increase without feeding inflation liming consumer price rises; illus

The Laws of Chance Explain Why the Pollster Is Probably Right
Article on widespread use of public opinion polls by people and industry to aid in decision making; theories of probability implicit in polls discussed by Profs John W Tukey, Mark Kac and Herbert Robbins; illus (L)

Suffolk Executive Warns: Hard Times Are Coming
Recent Nassau‐Suffolk Regional Planning Bd rept projects improvement in 2 counties’ econ (S)

Zero Traffic Growth Threatens Airlines
prices for gasoline in NYC met area have increased to between 53c and 58c because of fuel shortage (S)

People and Business
N J Nicholas Jr is apptd Sterling Manhattan Cable TV pres and chief exec officer, succeeding Richard M Galkin

MILD SLOWDOWN SEEN BY MOORE
Geoffrey Moore, BLS former comr and now of Natl Bur of Econ Research, analyzing outlook for US econ, sees only a mild slowdown in growth rate in ‘74; bases prediction on movement of 12 leading econ indicators, which go only through Nov, when energy crisis had not worked its way through statistics; discusses various facets of projection in Jan 11 int; says any discussion of energy shortage effect is only speculation at this point; characterizes current slowdown as ‘growth recession'; objection to that term by Econ Advisers Council chmn Dr H Stein noted; criteria for Moore and Natl Bur of Econ Research projection discussed; Use Mintz contributed to study; Moore por (L)

Two Surveys Cautious About Economy on L.I.
Recent LI Assn of Commerce and Indus and Franklin Natl Bank surveys show that energy crisis is expected to lower profits of LI business in ‘74 but econ outlook for Nassau and Suffolk (NY) Counties is cautiously optimistic; surveys’ results outlined; assn exec dir Fred E Merrill comments

After the appeasernent of the sheiks—The idea of Europe runs out of gas
Illus

Home Builders’ Very Own Recession
Housing indus is having recession and housing starts in US were at annual rate of 1.36‐million in Dec ‘73, down from 1.7‐million in Nov ‘73 and from avg monthly rate of 2.4‐million in 1st qr of ‘73; repercussions are being felt in appliances, lumber, carpeting, furniture and construction employment; home sales dropped drastically in last half of ‘73 when interest on mortgages climbed to 9% or more in some areas; construction materials have also climbed in price and land values have gone up; in addition some bldg materials are in short supply; builders across US comment; George C Martin, pres of Govt Natl Mortgage Assn, says Govt is slow to realize that nation is heading toward housing crisis; illus; graphs showing number of housing starts from Dec ‘72 through Dec ‘73 and rise in mortgage rates from Dec ‘72 through Dec ‘73 (L)

POINT OF VIEW; Inflation (Not Oil) as the Real Culprit; Strategy Requires Difficult Choices
Henry Kaufman article predicts continued high interest rates even if energy shortage is alleviated (M)

Africa, Her Hopes Dashed, Faces Uncertainty
survey on African econ outlook says higher world oil prices threaten recent econ gain; notes that in addition to higher oil costs, Africa will face increased prices for imports from indus nations seeking to offset own increased oil bills (Internatl Econ Survey) (M)

Plywood Producer Up
Continental Can Co chmn Robert S Hatfield repts record results for 4th qr and of all of ‘73; 4th qr net income rose 13.3% on sales and operating revenues increased by 22.2%; net earnings for yr gained 17.8% (S)

Chairman of Council Says Target Date Is Now Autumn
Econ Advisers Council Chmn Herbert Stein delays until Sept 1, possibly sooner, his resignation and acceptance of professorship at Univ of Va; Stein's relationship with Treas Sec Shultz noted; Stein por (M)

Lessened Prosperity Seen
Edward M Bernstein and Otto Eckstein (Data Resources Inc) have each issued a major analysis of impact of sharply higher oil prices on world econ; conclude that impact on US will depend on how other industrialized countries react and how new money of Arab oil producers is recycled; agree that if nations manipulated exch rates and trade policies in way that would 'beggar-their-neighbor,' US would bear brunt of deficit with oil-exporting countries; differ on extent to which Arab investment of new oil revenues can maintain equilibrium in world econ (M)

People and Business
United Artists Pictures Corp repts film Papillon has grossed more than $18-million

Inflation's Uncertainty Haunts Consumers
Program dir of Mich Univ's Survey Research Center Prof F Thomas Juster says outlook for consumer sector in '74 is gloomy; says consumers arc uncertain over inflation's course, and thus arc uncertain about their spending plans; says that if consumers were certain of inflation rate of, say, 6%, they would spend money faster than experts are predicting for '74; says consumers could learn to live with high inflation rate if they could see it coming and plan accordingly; visits NY to introduce new qrly pub, Econ Outlook USA, which will draw heavily on Survey Research Center's findings; illus (M)

Open Market Unit Voted Some Easing In Monetary Policy
Fed Reserve's Open Mkt Com voted with 1 dissent for modest easing of monetary policy, summary of Dec meeting released Feb 19; also agreed to place more emphasis on 'money-mkt conditions' such as short-term interest rates, and less on money supply growth (M)

DeButts of A.T.&T. Expresses ‘Guarded Optimism’
AT&T chmn John D deButts, in Feb 27 speech to NY Soc of Security Analysts, expresses 'guarded optimism' about '74 econ outlook (S)

Index Up Sharply in January, Off for December
Commerce Dept repts index of leading indicators rose 2% in Jan, biggest gain since Aug '72, but that Dec index, earlier reptd as gain of 0.1%, has been revised to 2.7% decline, sharpest drop since June '51; erratic movement in index underlines confusion over econ outlook; indicators suggest leveling off of econ activity at best; elements of strength reflect mainly inflationary pressures; graph (M)

Sudden Spanish Crackdown Shakes Thin Hopes for Some Liberalization
article discusses recent pol crisis in Spain resulting from energy shortage, execution of Catalan anarchist Salvador Puig Antich, despite thousands of clemency pleas, and police detention of Vizcaya Prov Bishop Antonio Anoveros Ataun, who called for greater freedom for Basques; events prompted occasional rioting by Barcelona and Madrid Univ students; other incidents contributing to unrest include '72 pol case in which 113 Govt oppositionists were arrested for holding 'illegal assembly' in Barcelona ch bldg and '72 refusal by Justice Min to allow 2 candidates for Madrid Bar Assn pres to be listed on ballots; events seen as reasons for skepticism about new Premier Carlos Arias Navarro's promised pol changes delivered in Feb 12 speech describing elective system of mayors and prov assembly heads, new pol and labor assns and clearer separation of Parliament from Govt; Govt's sudden show of toughness toward dissident groups also contributes to skepticism; pol attitudes in Madrid compared to those already prevalent in Barcelona; map (L)

ECONOMIC OUTLOOK HELD POOR IN STATE
NJ Labor and Indus Dept issues pessimistic rept on state's econ and predicts further slowdowns; Comr Joseph A Hoffman comments (S)
CREDIT ANALYSTS DIVIDED ON RATES
News Summary and Index; WEDNESDAY, MARCH 20, 1974

Britain on a Tightrope
ed, commenting on Brit Labor Govt's 1st budget, holds that it is ‘neutral pkg,’ aimed at reassuring domestic and foreign forces on which econ heavily depends while making some gestures toward Govt supporters and programs advocated in Feb; cites several Labor party programs passed, including removal from tax roll of 1.5‐million Britons at bottom of econ ladder, increasing taxes on corporate profits and higher personal income taxes; sees irony in Labor Govt being constrained to cut deficit spending by some $3.5‐billion and having to float large loan; hails Chancellor of the Exchequer Denis Healey's ability to handle situation

The Liner France, Running at a Loss, Is to End Service
French Transport Min Aymar Achille‐Fould announces liner France will be taken out of service, probably within next 6 wks to end operating deficit aggravated by increased oil prices; Chinese Nationalist Govt offer of $10‐million, scrap value, is only reptd offer thus far; ship illus (M)
The Liner Fraitce, Ranning at a Loss, Is to End Service

People and Business
PVO Internatl exec Frederic W Hammersfahr says one of most important factors in expanding US trade with USSR and China will be licensing of tech, speech, Licensing Execs Soc, NYC; notes licensing has potential for letting one enter foreign mkts and can become basis for highly profitable joint ventures (S)
Bearish Economic outlook Sends Stock Prices Dow

Business Briefs
Algerian state-owned co Sonatrach increases price of oil from $3.60 to $5 a bbl; under new system, co will announce oil price on 3-mo basis, taking into acct changes in world oil mkt and monetary field

Year of Twin Crises
econ analysis of factors influencing NY stock mkts holds uncertainty about impact of US shortage and Arab embargo on business imparts uncertain feeling among securities purchasers

Prices of Stocks Plummet; Dow Industrials Sink 24.24
energy crisis and Arab oil export embargo cited as factors in 24.24 drop in Dow Jones indus avg

The Crucial Capital Spending Boom
capital expenditures for petroleum refining capacity expected to rise; recently announced plans for new facilities still will not provide adequate capacity; US econ seen capable of weathering oil shortage if supplies are distributed with reasonable efficiency

‘Sluggish Growth’ Is Seen For Economy Next Year
Forum of Conference Bd on Nov 13 predicts that severe cutback in energy supplies will pose substantial threat to business conditions in '74; forum sees '74 as yr of discontent, uncertainty and sluggish growth resulting from energy crisis

Stock Prices Plunge 29.05 In Biggest Drop Since 1962
investor fears that energy crisis may exert econ slowdown in '74 spur drop in stock prices on Nov 26 mkt with Dow Jones plummeting 29.05 points to close at 824.95, biggest 1-day drop since May 28 '62

HIGH RISK IS SEEN IN 74 FORECASTS
Morgan Guaranty Trust Co '74 econ prognosis says prospects for basing predictions for new yr are flimsier than ever because of uncertain fuel picture and greater uncertainty about its impact on buying and other consumer variables
‘INFLATION IS SEEN IN COMMODITIES

Economic Analysis Group Wants Plan Used Abroad
Chilean Central Bank announces that exchange note for escudo has been adjusted to 405 escudos to $1 from 385; holds dollar-rate for tourists and exchange dealers unchanged at 750 escudos (S)

Market Place: Treasury Bills Now Yield 8%
Comment on attractiveness of investing in Treas bonds, (S)

G. E. Bomber Role Protested by Marchers
GE annual voting meeting is field in Chicago on Apr 24; defeats 5 soc or environmental proposals; chmn Reginald H Jones chides ch groups that submitted 4 or 5 proposals; says co is seeking woman or non-Caucasian for bd of dirs; Amer Friends Service Com and Clergy and Laity Concerned picket meeting in protest against co's role in bldg jet engines for B1 bomber; illus of Jones and sr vp Robert M Estes (M)

WASHINGTON REPORT
article discusses various econ paradoxes caused by oil shortage; predicts '74 will be combination of seriously short supply in some parts of econ and deficient demand in others; describes impact of energy crisis on some industry sectors, resulting in spot layoffs and price increases; discusses econ forecasts and sense of public confusion and bewilderment; illus

Oil and the Economy
L Silk says every Amer corp wants to know how energy crisis will affect it; says econ forecasting team headed by F P Murphy is making study for GE, using input-output model based on work of Prof W W Leontief; study's predictions of impact on US econ of Arab oil embargo are based on certain assumptions: that Arab state will yield to intense pressure and resume oil shipments to US by June '74, that curtailment of oil shipments to Japan and Western Eur will not be escalated further and may be eased and that Fed spending will increase enough to offset fiscal impact of any tax increases that may arise from fuel shortages; concludes that energy crisis is serious but manageable; estimates shortfall of 2.1 million bbls daily for 1st qr '74, about 11% less than normal demand of 19.5 million bbls; forecasts more extreme shortages in fuel oils (boiler fuel, diesel fuel and heating oil), which it says will fall 21% short of demand; estimates that mfg sector of econ as whole will suffer deficit of 14% of its normal fuel oil requirements in 1st qr '74, with larger shortfall in New England, 30%, and Middle Atlantic states, 20%; say lack of rail cars, barges, ships, storage tanks and unloading facilities will limit transshipments of any oil surpluses that may become available; cannot say how much fuel nation's mfrs have hoarded and can use to keep plants operating if oil supplies run lower than expected; on basis of relatively hopeful assumptions about mid-'74 end of Arab oil embargo, GE economists expect impact of energy crisis on production to be steep, short and highly selective, with heaviest damage to consumer outlays and indus spending, especially on cars, trucks and transportation-related services; say that if embargo were extended throughout '74, econ would go into deep recession, with GNP falling in all 4 qrs and unemployment rate climbing to 8% in 3d qr and to 8.5% in 4th; but think fear of backlash from Western countries will prevent Arabs from extending oil cuts and embargo that long

BUSINESS ROUND‐UP
fuel crisis has hit gasoline pump mfrs, forcing layoffs at Tokheim Corp and cutting production drastically at Bowser Pumps div of Keene Corp; only 491 service stations opened in 1st half of ‘73, down from 785 a yr earlier, but Gasoline Pumps Mfrs Assn pres George P Byrne predicts obsolescence and attrition to stop decline; mfrs are diversifying and trying to turn fuel criris to their benefit by mktg pumps that dispense pre‐set allocations automatically (S)

LETTERS
Lrs by Sam I Nakagama and Mrs Mable Innes Wallich on Soviet Prof S M Menshikov's Jan 6 article on US econ, predicting continued inflation and low GNP growth in '74; Mrs Wallich discusses closeness of his forecast to Amer consensus forecast; drawing

Is Budget Deficit Big Enough?
Charles L Schultze article on changes in econ as factor behind differences in Pres Nixon's fiscal '74 and fiscal '75 budgets; notes options Pres had in drawing up '75 budget and reasons for course he chose; holds there will be actual budget deficit of $9-billion; cartoon

Point of View
econ analyst Morris Cohen revs ‘73 stock mkt and predicts ‘74 performance; his illus (National Econ Survey) (M)

Beanie's ‘In House’ Work Policy Is Debated
Mayor Beame adm promise to have architectural and engineering work done by dept 'in-house' tech personnel, in effort to strengthen Civil Service, seen as almost certain to increase Civil Service ranks at time of extreme budget-consciousness (S)

General Tax Cut Opposed by Burns; Longer Executive‐Pay Curbs Asked
Fed Reserve Chmn Burns tells HR Appropriations com that easier Fed Reserve money and credit policy, appropriate for typical recession, would not help current problem because of unusual factor of oil shortage; his illus (S)
MARKETS IN REVIEW

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
tough approach is advocated by those who think energy emergency will be lengthy; official Govt stance is that crisis will be transitory; crisis has begun to make impact on certain industries although full effects will not be felt until winter weather comes; economists responses to queries on how to handle crisis vary

Stock Prices Slide On Recession Fears
NYSE trading vol is 16,120,000 shares on Jan 10; Dow Jones indus avg drops 11.68 points to close at 823.11 amid fears of recession in ‘74, huge increases in energy costs and high inflation rates (M)

Vanishing Controls
ed calling on Cong and Nixon Adm to fashion new anti-inflation machinery notes prime rate has broken 10% barrier and still tighter money is in prospect

Oil‐Supply Situation Spurs Suggestion
former Asst Sec R N Gardner on Nov 14 urges carefully drawn amendments to current trade bill to put oil-exporters on notice they cannot wage econ war on US with impunity; illus

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Article discusses rising militancy of labor wage negotiations and threat of increased strikes in '74; holds that relatively quiet and agreeable yr on labor front is turning into rather tense one in waning mos of '73, posing worrisome threat for '74, when large number of contract renewals come up in several key industries

Fed May Be Sharply Expanding Credit Supply, Analysts Assert
credit mkt analysts believe Fed Reserve may have initiated vigorous expansion of credit supply, spurred by energy shortage that has made an econ downturn more likely in 74; latest Reserve data do not reflect any massive stimulation of econ but they are entirely consistent with credit expansion; money supply shows avg annual rate of gain of 6.5% for latest 2 mos, well ahead of growth rate of 2.5% for most recent 4 mos and higher than 5.5% figure in latest 12; Reserve figures also reveal that short-term interest rates were somewhat easier in period ended Nov 28, that amt of outstanding commercial paper rose by $1.7-billion in wk ended Nov 21, that business loans at major NYC banks rose by $84-million to 2-wk total of $198-million; other figures

Energy: Not One Crisis, But Many
article on econ ramifications of energy crisis; age of continually rising expectations for Amers has been halted, at least temporarily; increasingly people are beginning to realize that more of everything is not always attainable simply with tech or money (Natl Econ Survey article) (L)
For Latins: 2d Industrial Revolution

India, Pakistan, Bangladesh On the Edge of Hunger
article on econ problems confronting India, Pakistan and Bangladesh; problems have grown as result of floods in Pakistan and drought in India; plight of typical Indian peasant described; illus of field under cultivation in India and Pakistani refugees from floods (Internat Econ Survey article) (L)

Selling Drive Hailed As Army Tops Goal For National Guard
Army Natl Guard repts enlistments for Jan '74 rose to authorized level, bringing total enrollment to 403,600; says enlistments are up partially through increase in drill pay (S)

‘Do Something!’
W Safire article on Nixon's pronouncement 'there will be no recession' and on concern by his aides Treas Sec George P Shultz and Econ Advisers Council chmn Herbert Stein on Nixon's decision for pol reasons to 'do something'; recalls Shultz confrontation with Sen William Proxmire over need for 'action'; questions wisdom of doing almost anything to avoid a recession, citing effects on inflation

Aliens Near Paris Still a Breed Apart 6 Years After the Rioting
Article on adverse living conditions of alien labor force in North African neighborhood of Nanterre, France; majority of 3-million workers are North African immigrants, with remaining dwellers consisting of Algerians and Moroccans; plight of aliens search for decent housing and over-all decent living conditions discussed; illus

Market Place
Comment on resurgence of supermkt chains stocks after yr when earnings and stocks were sliding (M)

RCA Earnings Drop 17% in First Period
RCA repts earnings for 1st qr of '74 fell to $34.8-million, from $41.7-million recorded in 1st qr of '73; repts sales rose to $1.08-billion against previous high of $1.01-billion a yr earlier; chmn Robert W Sarnoff comments on earnings decline; por (M)

Chimera of Controls
John T Connor, Commerce Sec in Johnson Adm and now chief exec of Allied Chem, says he welcomes end of wage-price controls; says they became unjust to indus; says we are coming out of econ downturn much faster than anticipated in Dec or Jan; says real problem is excessive inflation; sees no need for fiscal stimulation of econ (S)

Outlook for Economy
Econ outlook analyzed; most forecasters see improvement by yr end; unprecedented simultaneous occurrence of decline in production and increase in prices noted; most forecasters see production decline ending, output slowly rising this yr; see prices continuing to rise, but at slower rate than 14.5% annual rate in 1st qr, particularly food and fuel; Dr Arthur M Okun says '74 will qualify as recession yr, but not very deep; sees inflation rate of 6% at yr end; end of severe collapse in auto sales and output seen as sign that econ downturn has run its course and signs of recovery are beginning to surface (M)

Washington Ice Age
Ed deplores 'glacial immobility' of econ policy makers in Nixon Adm and in Cong as wage-price controls program expires; notes moves in Cong for limited extension but doubts extension can be achieved; says reality is that both parties appear unable or unwilling to undertake any comprehensive initiatives either to curb inflation or ward off recession; sees whole job left to Fed Reserve through its control of monetary policy, with risk of steeper econ downturn without halting rise in prices

Nominee as Adviser Emphasizes Need for Consistency
Prof W J Fellner says controlling inflation requires consistent Govt policy of forcing slowdowns in economy whenever inflation rate exceeds pre-established acceptable level, speech Oct 10 at Fed Statistics Users conf, Washington; stresses need for Govt follow-through on slowdown threats to establish credibility; says credibility engenders voluntary restraint; former chmn of Econ Advisers Council A M Okun, also speaking at conf, optimistic on future price trends; says '73 inflation was not caused by errors in Govt policies but by supply and demand imbalances; Fellner illus

State Says Economy Will: Slow Up a Bit And Then Recover
NJ Labor and Indus Comr R M Heymann, asserting on Oct 13 that NJ will go through 'mild econ slowdown' in next 2 yrs, notes that level of unemployment should remain essentially unchanged at present rate of 7% during econ readjustment

Business Briefs
Eur aircraft mfrs on Oct 23 ask EEC for urgent help in their struggle to obtain more business; reptdly want community to adopt Eur aeronautical policy fully supported by member govts so that indus can become more competitive

DUTCH ASK HELP AS ARABS CUT Off
Netherlands Govt bans pleasure driving to conserve fuel; Min M van der Stoel asserts embargo is intended to force Western Eur to take pro-Arab line, news conf; says action was coordinated prior to outbreak of war; Min R Lubbers, in lr outlining conservation measures to Parliament, calls for reduction of heating, car pooling and maintaining 60-mph speed limit; officials hope 65-day oil reserve maintained by refineries will not have to be used; concede greatest danger is econ slowdown, not oil shortage

COPPER FUTURES DROP DAILY LIMIT
new limitations on copper trading for Dec contracts ends recent sharp rise in price on NY Commodity Exch; contracts drop on Nov 21 to $1.02 85/100, compared with $1.05 85/100 on Nov 20

People and Business
Israeli Econ Min A Sher on Nov 21 predicts that there will be rise in foreign investment in Israel, int; says that Middle East peace settlement will widen export opportunities in Middle East; says Israel's econ is rapidly recovering from recent war

European Scene
comment on econ dislocations in W Eur and threatened recession from Arab oil embargo

Churchman Sees Need to Curb Extremism in Radio
ABC pres E H Rule on Dec 11 predicts that TV ad revenues will increase in '74, speech to seminar sponsored by NY Soc of Security Analysts and Ad Age; says over 70% of ABC's prime-time schedule is sold out for almost entire 1st 3 qrs; cites energy crisis as another factor that will contribute to TV's revenue growth; says because of crisis, more people will remain home and watch TV

Sputtering Western Economies
Article says industrialized Western world, which has produced unrivaled period of gen prosperity for world, despite pockets of poverty, is now facing severe dislocations because of embargoes and cutbacks by Arab oil producers; notes world is expected to be much different place even if oil starts flowing again; says skyrocketing cost of energy will mean that everyone may be little poorer; notes Eur, US and Japan showed signs of approaching recession even before Middle East war broke out; holds confluence of oil crisis and downswing in business cycle, hitting all major Western indus countries at once, has concerned econ mgrs, caused worries about job security, created bear mkt in stock exchs and compounded hazards of forecasters' jobs; says deteriorating business climate in Western world is coupled with dangers of spreading econ nationalism and protectionism if govts do not work closely together to find common solutions; notes financial mins of major Western powers met 3 wks ago to try to plan monetary and financial strategy over difficult period ahead, but says little progress was made except in promoting exch-rate stability

Light Amid the Gloom
comment on econ outlook in light of energy crisis finds reasons for optimism despite seriousness of problems; suggests possibility that problems are being overexaggerated and that ingenuity in coping with them is being underestimated; notes surprising cooperation of business and pub in conserving energy, alacrity of Washington in dealing with energy crisis, and a better understanding of economy's problem, in business and Govt, with passage of time; cites views expressed by former chmn of Econ Advisers Council P W McCracken in an article last wk on relationship between econ growth and rise in energy consumption; McCracken says there has been an increase in productivity of energy, and that on the avg, there is more than 1% increase in output for each percentage point increase in energy use; notes wide variation in relationship of energy used and GNP from yr to yr; in '70, GNP declined 0.4% with 5.6% increase in energy used; in '71, GNP rose 3.2% with 0.4% increase in energy used

Economic Ills in Europe Stirring Fears of Portuguese Workers
Portuguese Pres A Thomaz comments on continuing attacks by Portugal's enemies against its efforts to hold on to its African colonies

Trudeau Says Canadians Won't Have Oil Shortage
Canadian Prime Min Trudeau on Dec 30 says that Canadians will not experience serious oil shortages and doubts energy crisis will lead to econ slowdown in '74

Earnings Decline Is Under Way
article by AT&T research aide Peter C Manus predicting 6‐7% profits drop in ‘74, with after‐tax drop of about 15%; comments on factors that distort profits picture; graph (Natl Econ Survey article) (L)

Paper Shortage Plagues Business
paper shortage that has plagued US business in ‘73 is expected to continue for some time; situation is so extreme that US Paperworkers Internatl Union in Dec bought ad in major newspapers to appeal to Govt to let pulp and paper prices be determined by mkt forces and not by Govt controls; Amer Paper inst pres E A Locke Jr says assuming that econ activity continues at its current pace, he does not see situation improving; Internatl paper Co execs have suggested that return on investment of 8% to 10%, rather than going rate of 5% before interest, would be needed to encourage newsprint makers to plunge into new ventures; Locke repts that indus spent $98‐million on primary facilities during ‘73 but that $480‐million of total was expended on pollution control equipment (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

The Nation
Admr Simon's prediction of lie per gal gasoline price rise by Mar revd

Black Mayors and Rights Chiefs Find a Sympathetic Ear in Ford
Nixon aide Stanley A Scott repts impact of oil shortage on natl econ was primary topic at Jan 11 meeting between group of 16 black majors and Vice Pres Ford that was arranged by Scott at Ford's behest (S)

Banks Are Rushing Abroad Pell‐Mell
NY Times survey on internationalization of banking finds latest movement is not toward traditional banking centers but toward rising new financial centers; details mechanics of multinatl banking arrangements; notes 1 innovation is Eurco, which is composite currency made from money from various EEC countries to deal with currency uncertainty; co trademarks illus (Internatl Econ Survey) (L)
Economic Clouds Thicken Overlberia
News Summary and Index TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 5, 1974

‘Judgmental Factors’ of a Recession
L Silk analysis of issue facing econ policy-makers in '74 of whether to deploy their forces against recession or inflation, both of which are being exacerbated by energy shortage; says issue has become somewhat confused by Nixon's assertion 'there will be no recession'; examines question of what constitutes a recession and quotes Treas Sec George P Shultz on that and related matters; also examines question whether Nixon, if he 'busts' budget to avoid recession, would simply worsen inflation (M)

MBFR Deadlock
ed on resumption of Vienna conf on mutual reduction of forces in Central Eur supports stiffening of NATO resistance to Soviet proposals that maintain and could even increase Warsaw Pact's present preponderance of power; holds key word is 'balance' if mil situation in Central Eur is to be stabilized

Nixon Sounds a Warning On Raw‐Material Prices
Pres Nixon tells Cong that oil countries on whole have large monetary reserves, 2d annual internatl econ rept (S)

Brimmer of Federal Reserve Finds Black Job Gains in 1973
Fed Reserve System member Andrew F Brimmer, in rept, says that blacks in '73 made marked gains in participation in labor force, over-all employment and expanded opportunities in higher-paying blue-collar and professional jobs; says there was moderate increase in black unemployment; says unemployment rate for blacks increased by 1.2% since recent low in Oct, to 9.6% in Jan; says employment outlook in '74 for black workers is pessimistic because of econ slowdown and energy crisis; recommends higher funding of public employment programs, extension of unemployment ins benefits and special training programs that are under consideration by Cong to ease impact on black workers; repts increase of black participation in labor force was most prominent among women and young workers; there were 10-million blacks looking for or holding jobs in '73, up 330,000 from '72; jobs held by blacks increased by 400,000 to 9.3-million (M)

Nixon Sounds a Warning On Raw‐Material Prices
Pres Nixon's 2d annual internatl econ rept contains discussion on world food problems; finds there is considerable disagreement as to whether current food gap is permanent; states that most agr experts believe that current situation will continue in foreseeable future (S)
MARKETS IN REVIEW

The Nation
Anthony Austin and Milton Leebaw comment noting US Sen's failure to ratify UN Genocide Conv because of filibuster staged by Southern Sens
LESSER CALENDAR OF FINANCING SET

NET UP 14% IN '73 AT GENERAL TEL
Gen Telephone & Electronics Corp repts '73 total sales and revenues rose to $5.11-billion; revenues from phone operations rose to $2.5-billion while manufacturing sales totaled $2.6-billion
Stock List Inches Ahead But Glamour Issues Sag

Business Briefs
Statistics Canada wheat rev shows that Canadian wheat exports fell to 187.1-million bu during 1st 5 mos of '73 crop yr, down 24% from corresponding '72 period (S)

West Germany at the Crossroads
C L Sulzberger article, asserting W Ger Chancellor Brandt's efforts to improve relations with USSR, E Ger and other E Eur nations seem to have stalled, notes widespread concern in W Ger that fuel crisis will spur econ recession

Setbacks for Mr. Heath
ed on upcoming Brit elections holds that Prime Min Heath and Conservative party, thought to be headed for decisive victory, are faced with new developments that could make election outcome uncertain; new developments include Natl Pay Bd's discovery of calculation error that caused Govt to exaggerate pay of striking coal miners and Conservative MP Enoch Powell's encouragement to Britons to support Labor party because of promises to renegotiate terms of Brit membership in EEC
BURNS IS PARK TO ‘YIELD RALLY’

Republican Governors Looking to Ford
Meets, Washington, DC

Unemployed Rate at 5.2%, Halting 3‐Month Increase
Labor Dept analysis, published on Mar 8, finds about 500,000 workers have lost their jobs because of oil shortage; finds majority of job loss in airline and garage industries (S)

Unemployed Rate at 5.2%, Halting 3‐Month Increase
US BLS repts avg work week in Feb in mfg and entire private econ rose after sizable drop in Jan; notes work week in mfg was 40.5-hrs, up from 40.2-hrs in Jan (S)

Market Place: Oil Anticipation Outruns Action
repts that Arab nations will end oil embargo against US has only mild effect on Wall Street; several analysts believe embargo never had severely negative impact on Amer econ, but acknowledge that higher fuel prices resulting from embargo will be factored into inflation (S)

Impact of Embargo Lingers On
article on longrange impact of Arab oil policies on US and world econ (M)

Shultz Opposes Tax Cut Proposals as Inflationary
Treas Sec Shultz recommends enactment of improvements in unemployment benefits that Nixon Adm has proposed as means of dealing with rising unemployment, testimony, Sen Finance Com (S)

Bankers Trust Co. Increases Its Prime Rate to 9%
traders in fixed‐income securities mkts say Fed Reserve seems to have shifted in subtle way to promote slightly higher interest rates; interest rates on Fed funds have been moving upward for several wks as central bank has seemed disinclined to stop rise; funds rate averaged 9.03% during wk ended Mar 13, up from 8.98% one wk earlier; graph shows Fed funds rate, Feb ‘73 to Feb ‘74 (S)

U.S. Economists Divided On How to Curb Inflation
Survey on causes of US inflation notes majority of economists believe Govt must stop trying to drive econ so fast, 5% unemployment, which is roughly considered to be 'full'; adds efforts to get joblessness below 5% must rely on public-service employment programs for hard-to-employ; notes that creation of new, low-skilled public-service jobs has met resistance within govt, but outside conservative economists seem to be increasingly in favor of idea (S)

U.S. Economists Divided On How to Curb Inflation
Survey on causes of US inflation cites world-wide boom conditions that sent food and other commodity prices soaring (S)

To Cure Slumpflation
Ed lauds Brit Chancellor of the Exchequer Denis Healey econ budget to stave off inflation; points out that Healey's budget is 'broadly neutral,' seeking to restrain aggregate demand and seeking 'soc compact' between Govt and trade unions; notes that new budget will increase taxes by $3.3-billion and raise prices charged by nationalized industries; holds that critical test will be whether Prime Min Wilson can gain unions' support for moderation in wage demands
U.S. JOBLESS RATE STEADY IN MARCH FOR THIRD MONTH

U.S. JOBLESS RATE STEADY IN MARCH FOR THIRD MONTH
US BLS repts nation's unemployment rate in Mar was 5.1%, decline from 5.2% in Feb; Govt and private economists expect rise in jobless rate in '74 as result of current decline in econ; official Govt forecast, repeated this wk by Council of Econ Advisers Chmn Stein, is that '74 unemployment will probably go above 5.5%; many private forecasters expect 6% or higher unemployment rate by yr's end; total employment figure based on household survey was 85.86-million in Mar, compared with 85.8-million in Feb; alternate figure, based on payroll repts by employers, showed decline in Mar to 76.65-million from 76.77-million in Feb; Stein, while indicating that unemployment rate may rise somewhat in future mos, says Mar rept tends to support view that econ slowdown will be of moderate depth and duration; total unemployment in Mar was 4,633,000, seasonally adjusted; new statistical series on employment situation of persons of Spanish origin is published in rept; shows that in 1st qr of "74 their unemployment rate was 8.4%, well above 5.1% figure for whites but below 9.8% for Negroes; graph shows fluctuations in unemployment, Jan '71-Mar '74; chart shows total employment and unemployment figures, June '73-Mar 74 (M)

FOUR‐YEAR PERIOD OP JOB LOSS HERE IS BELIEVED OVER
NY Times survey of professional economists' views on NYC econ; notes they agree that econ apparently has ended 4-yr period of job losses and that whatever job growth is achieved in next few yrs will come in smaller increments and will be significantly slower than natl job-growth avg; economists Mrs Karen Gerard of Chase Manhattan Bank, George Roniger of First Natl City Bank and Benjamin Slome of Manufacturers Hanover Trust Co and Econ Development Admr Alfred Eisenpreis comment; economists appear cautiously optimistic because of uncertainty over causes of sustained job loss and impact of steep rise in energy costs during '73; problems discussed; Econ Development Adm officials feel city's attractiveness as place to do business has been enhanced by building of huge amt of office space (L)
FOUR‐YEAR PERIOD OF JOB LOSS HERE IS BELIEVED OVER

The Nation
rev of week's events in Mitchell-Stans trial; Mitchell illus (M)

UPWARD PRESSURE ON RATES REMAINS
Fed Reserve repts rise in short-term interest rates and business loan demand in week ended Apr 17 (M)

The Game Plan After Controls: A. Lot of the Old‐Time Religion
article notes that Fed Reserve Bd Chmn Burns has moved to check rate of increase in money supply and to cut business borrowings in effort to halt inflation; says that Fed must take acct of past inflation without increasing money supply so rapidly as to fuel further inflation and must also try to bring down rate of inflations without precipitating major econ slowdown
CALENDAR HEAVY IN DEBT MARKETS

HOUSE VOTES RISE IN FUNDS TO HI
HR votes, 339-21, to increase SBA's lending authority by $575-million until June 30 '74; HR Small Business subcom adds stiff provision prohibiting 'improper influence' from forces outside SBA and continues its investigation of allegations ranging from 'possible criminal activities to poor mgt' in 21 SBA field offices; Repr E I Koch argued against passage of bill, saying investigation should be completed and safeguards added before passage; subcom chmn Repr R G Stephens predicts that investigation will necessitate writing of new SBA legis in next 6 mos

Good‐by to Controls ...
Ed on Nixon Adm lifting of wage-price controls holds that only bright spot in anti-inflation picture has been continence of organized labor in face of sharp ascent in living costs; sees 3% decrease in purchasing power spurring rank-and-file demands in '74 for bigger wage boosts or for universal adoption of escalator clauses

Mr. Perón's Burden
ed says that newly inaugurated Argentine Pres J D Peron has failed to bring under control disparate forces within his movement; notes continued acts of terrorism and pol assassinations; says unless urban terror is stopped, foreign firms will hesitate to send men and money needed by ailing Argentine econ

CHARLES BOHLEN, DIPLOMAT,69,DIES
Bohlen, Charles E
CHARLES BOHLEN, DIPLOMAT,69,DIES

Point of View
Albert H Cox Jr comment on econ policies says traditional fiscal and monetary policies are unconnected with Arab oil embargo; says econ policy makers will have to continue their study of US energy problems to insure reasonable growth because Arabs probably will not provide sufficient energy after embargo is lifted; says Fed tax benefits for energy conservation and penalties for wastage are probable; asserts cumulative effect of US anti‐pollution laws may acct for entire energy shortfall and must be evaluated in that light; asserts price US will pay for cleaner environment will be in jobs (Natl Econ Survey) (M)

Wonderful You and Me
R Baker comments on new Canadian record by Gordon Sinclair which is selling at astonishing pace and which delcares that Amers are marvelous people but nobody appreciates them; compares tone of 4‐min editorial to Prof Higgins in My Fair Lady in ‘50s

Crossroads for Chile
ed on pol situation in Chile lauds leaders of Christian Dems for writing lr to ruling junta appealing for country's freedom; deplores repression and lack of civil liberties in Chile, and warns that junta would make colossal mistake if it patterns its pol behavior after that of dictators in other South Amer countries; predicts junta will lose essential personnel, and US Govt and others will face public pressures to hold back on aid, unless pol climate improves; hopes junta will opt for gradual restoration of democracy

Probing the Market's Depths
Indicators of past stock mkt declines discussed with view of placing present decline in perspective; bear mkts of '6162, '66 and '68-70 assessed; Merrill Lynch vp Robert J Farrell's contention that odd-lot, short-sale ratio was most consistent indicator in past 3 cycles, delineating approximate mkt bottom, noted; mutual funds' cash position as percentage of their total net assets seen as another good indicator of mkt bottoms in '66 and '70; fact that bottom areas for bear mkts of '60s showed high degree of correlation with number of daily new lows posted on NYSE noted; graph showing Dow Jones indus avg during declines of '60s and during current decline (L)

NATO at Twenty‐Five ...
ed on outlook for France following Pompidou's death sees more conciliatory attitude toward US and France's EEC partners should Gaullist Jacques Chaban-Delmas or Repub Ind Min Valery Giscard d'Estaing succeed him

Edwin Nourse, 90, Dies; Truman's Economic Aide
Nourse, Edwin G (Dr)

THE ECONOMIC SCENE Clash on Taxes and Controls
necessity for Fed Reserve to keep firm rein on monetary growth at present time discussed; Fed must keep money tight but not so terribly restrictive that it pushes interest rates significantly higher and thus aborts econ recovery predicted by most economists for 2d half of '74
HIGHLIGHTS OF THE WEEK

Economists Plead Uncertainty on 1974 Outlook
Comment on forecasts for '74 finds economists wary because of their blunders in '73, the energy shortage, and uncertainty about the future; sees mild recession in 1st half of '74, with slight drop in GNP; sees econ activity speeding up in 2d half, with worse inflation and slight drop in profits; former chmn of Econ Advisers Council A M Okun says '73 error was 'one of the greatest failures of econ analysis in modern times'some economists feel they have over-emphasized macro-economics and neglected micro-economics, the study of industries and cos; this yr they are studying fuel

The Penitent Seers
L Silk econ analysis notes 70% or more of economists questioned in long‐term forecast believe degree of unionization in white‐collar and public‐sector occupations will move up dramatically (S)

G. E. Reports Profit Records
GE chmn Reginald H Jones on Jan 24 expects earnings will reach $585‐million, compared with $530‐million in ‘72, news conf; repts 4th qr income of $191‐million, compared with ‘72 4th qr profits of $177.2‐million; illus (M)
10‐POINT PROPOSAL

10‐POINT PROPOSAL
Nixon, in his State of Union Address to Cong Jan 30, outlines program for ‘74 he says will make new progress toward peace, stressing that his overriding aim has been and continues to be establishment of a ‘new structure of peace in world’ (S)

Outlines 7 Alternatives And Calls for Restraint
Treas Sec Shultz, at briefing on Nixon Adm's '75 budget, says 1 way to counteract recession is for Fed Reserve to follow 'classic' expansionist policy in supplying credit needs (S)

THE ECONOMIC SCENE
Comment on econ outlook and inflation; growing sense of unease, tension and discontent over state of econ seen hardly warranted by over-all performance and prospects, but grave concern over pattern of price indexes is seen justified; inflation called Public Enemy No. 1

Nigerians, Rejecting Rural Lives, Flock to Capital
current scene, Lagos (Nigeria), described; some urban problems outlined; illus (L)
